title: Loops
description: The overview before for, while, and do-while: why repetition needs its own tool, and the pieces every loop shares.
objectives: Understand why repeating code by hand doesn't scale
Understand the three pieces every loop needs: a starting point, a condition, and a way to move forward
Understand what an infinite loop is and why it happens
Preview for, while, and do-while as the upcoming subtopics

sections.text: This prints 1 through 5, but it's five separate lines for a simple, repetitive pattern. Now imagine needing 1 through 1,000 — copy-pasting a thousand cout lines clearly doesn't scale.
A loop is a control structure that repeats a block of code multiple times, without writing that code out more than once.

sections.title: To repeat something a controlled number of times, a loop needs
points: A starting point — where the repetition begins (e.g. a counter starting at 1)
A condition — checked before each repetition, deciding whether to continue (e.g. counter <= 5)
A way to move forward — something that changes each time, so the condition eventually becomes false (e.g. counter++)

steps: True → run the block, then move forward, check again
False → stop, continue after the loop

sections.text: This mirrors what we've already used: a counter is a variable, the condition is a bool expression built from comparison operators, and "moving forward" is often ++, all covered in earlier lessons.

sections.title: Doing push-ups
sections.text: "Do 10 push-ups" doesn't mean writing out ten separate instructions — it means: start a counter at 0, repeat one push-up while the counter is less than 10, and increase the counter by 1 each time. That's exactly the shape of a loop: a start, a condition, and a step forward.
4. What Happens If the Condition Never Becomes False?

If a loop's condition is never checked correctly, or the counter never actually moves it toward false, the loop runs forever — an infinite loop. The program appears to freeze, endlessly repeating the same block. We'll see exactly how this happens (and how to avoid it) once we write real loop code next.

sections.title: C++ provides a few different loop structures
points: for — best when you know how many times to repeat, or have a clear counter
while (a future topic) — best when repetition depends on a condition, not a fixed count
do-while (a future topic) — like while, but always runs the block at least once

sections.text: This lesson is only the concept. for's exact syntax is covered next; while and do-while will follow in their own topics.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting to move the counter forward — without it, the condition never changes, and the loop never ends.
Getting the condition backward — a condition that's false from the very start means the loop body never runs at all.
Assuming every repetition problem needs the same loop type — the right choice depends on whether the number of repetitions is known ahead of time.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is a loop?
questions.answer: A control structure that repeats a block of code multiple times without writing that code out more than once.

questions.question: What three things does a loop generally need?
questions.answer: A starting point, a condition checked before each repetition, and a way to move forward so the condition can eventually become false.

questions.question: What is an infinite loop?
questions.answer: A loop whose condition never becomes false, so it keeps repeating forever instead of stopping.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A loop repeats a block of code multiple times without duplicating that code. Every loop generally needs a starting point, a condition checked before each repetition, and a way to move forward so the condition eventually becomes false — if that last part is missing, the result is an infinite loop that never stops. C++ provides a few loop structures for different situations: for, typically used when the number of repetitions is known, and while and do-while, better suited when repetition depends purely on a condition.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Loops
sections.text: Every loop = Start, Check, Run, Move forward, repeat. Forget to move forward, and it never stops.
